summary: I recall seeing the Tulsa World newspaper on July 27th, which announced PSO's $1.3 billion wind energy investment. That headline really set the stage for understanding the energy issues we were facing. Tulsa World was a key source for local news and helped me keep track of important developments in Oklahoma's energy sector.

PSO played a big role in my story, especially when they announced a massive wind energy investment in 2017. Back in the early 1970s, PSO had bought nearly two thousand acres near Inola to build a nuclear plant, which sparked a lot of community concern. They tried to convince the residents about the benefits of nuclear power, but I was skeptical because of what I had read about radiation effects.

On July 27, 2017, USA Today had a front-page story about nuclear plant closures sending towns into crisis. That headline resonated with me because it reflected the challenges communities face when energy infrastructures change. It helped contextualize the struggles around nuclear power that I was involved with in Oklahoma.

I am Carrie Dickerson, a housewife who graduated from Oklahoma State University with a degree in education. I started as a teacher and later earned my master's while teaching in Tiawah, where I conducted research on vitamin deficiencies in children. I also taught Home Economics at Inola High School and other schools like Talala and Chelsea, where I stood up for boys learning home economics. My activism grew from my concern about nuclear power plants near my community.

I attended Oklahoma State University where I earned my degree in education and later my master's. While there, I witnessed segregation firsthand when a black student was told to sit outside the classroom, and I stood with her in protest. OSU also holds a collection of my work and mementos related to my fight against the nuclear plant near Inola.

Tiawah is a small town south of Claremore where I taught school and did my master's research. During the winter there, fresh vegetables were scarce, and many students suffered from painful boils due to vitamin deficiencies. My experiment giving them vitamins A and D helped clear their skin, which was the basis of my thesis.

Claremore is near Tiawah, where I taught and did research in the early part of my career. I went to fourth grade there, so it was familiar territory for me. The proximity of Claremore helped shape my early experiences as an educator in Oklahoma.

I taught Home Economics at Inola High School after earning my master's degree. It was one of several schools where I worked, including Talala and Chelsea. Inola was also the community where the nuclear plant was planned, which deeply affected me and my activism.

Talala was one of the schools where I taught Home Economics. My work there was part of my broader teaching career across several Oklahoma towns. It gave me experience and perspective on rural education in the region.

In Chelsea, I had a memorable confrontation with the principal because I believed boys should learn Home Economics just like girls. I let boys into my classes, and they responded positively, especially while building a cottage in Shop class. Although the principal opposed this and I nearly lost my job, I stood firm and gained many supporters.

Oklahoma is where my entire story unfolds—from my education at OSU to teaching in towns like Tiawah, Inola, Talala, and Chelsea. It was also the state where PSO planned a nuclear plant near Inola, sparking my activism. Segregation was still present during my school years here, which I challenged directly.

Clara Dickerson was my twin sister, and she was very proud of the story I told about standing up against segregation at OSU. She was redheaded and often shared that story with others. Her pride in my actions meant a lot to me as we faced challenges together.

Inola is the town where the nuclear plant was planned in the early 1970s, which deeply impacted me and my family. I was the head nurse at the nursing home my husband and I built there. The community meetings with PSO and the breaking of ground for the plant were pivotal moments that motivated my activism.

During the time when Eisenhower was promoting Atoms for Peace, the idea of nuclear power was being sold as a positive force. This was shortly after World War II and the atomic bombings, which made me wary of nuclear technology. Eisenhower's policies influenced the context in which PSO planned the nuclear plant near Inola.

Atoms for Peace was a program promoted by President Eisenhower to encourage peaceful uses of nuclear energy. This was the backdrop when PSO proposed the nuclear plant near Inola. Although it sounded promising, I was concerned about the risks based on what I had read about radiation effects.

World War II had just ended with the atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki, events that deeply influenced my views on nuclear power. The war's end and the use of atomic bombs made me cautious about nuclear technology being introduced near my community. The history of the war was always in the background of the nuclear debates in Oklahoma.

entity: Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki
summary: The atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki were tragic events that shaped my fear of nuclear technology. Knowing the destructive power used in those bombings made me question the safety of nuclear plants like the one PSO wanted to build near Inola. These bombings were a constant reminder of the potential dangers.

Oak Ridge, Tennessee, was a key site for the research and construction of the atomic bombs during World War II. I read stories about deformities in animals around there, which made me fearful of radiation effects on my own family. This knowledge fueled my opposition to the nuclear plant project near Inola.
